tree_title: Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers :     Occupational Outlook Handbook: :     U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics
overall_topic: Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers :     Occupational Outlook Handbook: :     U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics
questions: Within federal U.S. labor statistics data, identify the management occupation that plans, coordinates, and directs computer-related activities in organizations and shares the same 2024–2034 projected growth rate (15%) as software developers and related testing professionals. Provide its 2024 median annual pay, typical entry-level education, required related work experience, and average annual job openings projected over the decade. Additionally, identify the academic field-of-degree category that encompasses majors preparing students for careers in computing and information technology, and state its most recent modification date along with at least three example majors listed under that category.

Identify the U.S. occupation group that includes professionals who design computer applications or programs as well as those who identify problems in software and report defects. For this combined occupational category, provide the following details: the 2024 median annual wage for the application-design role and for the quality assurance/testing role separately; the overall projected employment growth rate from 2024 to 2034; the total number of jobs in 2024; the typical entry-level education required; and the average number of projected annual openings over the decade. Summarize all findings clearly.

Identify the U.S. occupation group that combines professionals who design computer applications with those who test and identify defects in those applications. For this combined occupation group, provide the following: the 2024 median pay for the overall group, the separate 2024 median annual wages for the development role and for the quality assurance/testing role, the typical entry-level education required, the projected job growth rate for 2024–2034, the projected average annual openings over the decade, the total number of jobs in 2024, and the common industries or work environments in which these professionals are employed.

Identify the U.S. occupation group that encompasses professionals who design and build computer applications as well as those who systematically test software, identify defects, and report quality issues. Using the most recent federal labor market data (2024 estimates and 2024–2034 projections), summarize the following for this occupation group: the typical entry-level education requirement; whether related work experience or on-the-job training is generally required; the total number of jobs in 2024; the projected percent employment growth and numeric employment change from 2024 to 2034; the average number of projected annual openings; the 2024 median annual wages for the application-design role and for the testing/quality assurance role separately; and the common industries or work environments in which these professionals are employed.

Identify the U.S. occupation category that encompasses professionals who design computer applications or programs as well as those who identify defects and ensure the quality of those applications before release. For this combined occupational group, report the 2024 median annual pay, typical entry-level education requirement, number of jobs in 2024, projected employment growth rate for 2024–2034, average number of projected annual openings over the decade, and a brief description of their typical work environment.

Identify the U.S. occupation group that includes professionals who design computer applications as well as those who test and identify defects in software, and that is projected to grow 15% from 2024 to 2034 with nearly 1.9 million jobs recorded in 2024. For this occupational group, provide the following details: the 2024 median annual pay for application developers and separately for quality assurance analysts and testers; the typical entry-level education required; whether prior related work experience or on-the-job training is typically required; the total number of jobs in 2024; the projected employment change from 2024 to 2034; the average number of projected annual openings; and a description of their typical work environment.

Identify the U.S. occupation group that includes professionals who design computer applications or programs as well as those who test software and report defects. For this occupation group, provide a comprehensive profile that includes: the 2024 median annual pay (overall and broken out by role, if applicable), the typical entry-level education required, whether prior related work experience or on-the-job training is typically needed, the total number of jobs in 2024, the projected percent employment growth from 2024 to 2034, the numeric employment change over that decade, and the average number of annual job openings projected during the period. Present all figures clearly and specify which statistics apply to which role where relevant.

Identify the trio of closely related computing occupations that include professionals who design computer applications or programs and those who identify and report defects in those applications. For this occupational group in the United States, provide the following details: the 2024 median annual wage for the development role and for the quality assurance/testing role; the typical entry-level education required; whether prior related work experience or on-the-job training is generally required; the total number of jobs in 2024; the projected employment growth rate from 2024 to 2034 and the numeric employment change over that period; the average number of projected annual openings; and a description of their typical work environment, including common industries of employment.

Identify the U.S. occupation group that includes professionals who design computer applications or programs as well as those who test software to identify defects and ensure quality before release. For this combined occupational category, provide a detailed overview that includes: the 2024 median annual pay for each major role within the group, the typical entry-level education required, the total number of jobs in 2024, the projected employment growth rate for 2024–2034, the projected numeric employment change over that decade, the average number of annual openings, and a summary of their typical work environment. Compile all requested information into a single, coherent response.

Identify the U.S. occupation category that encompasses professionals who design computer applications or programs as well as those who test software, identify defects, and report problems. For this occupation group, provide the 2024 median annual pay for both developers and quality assurance analysts/testers separately, the typical entry-level education required, the total number of jobs in 2024, the projected percentage employment growth from 2024 to 2034, the average number of projected annual openings over the decade, and a summary of the typical work environment.

rounds.question: Within federal U.S. labor statistics data, identify the management occupation that plans, coordinates, and directs computer-related activities in organizations and shares the same 2024–2034 projected growth rate (15%) as software developers and related testing professionals. Provide its 2024 median annual pay, typical entry-level education, required related work experience, and average annual job openings projected over the decade. Additionally, identify the academic field-of-degree category that encompasses majors preparing students for careers in computing and information technology, and state its most recent modification date along with at least three example majors listed under that category.
checklist_width: 2024 median annual pay for the management occupation ($171,200)
Typical entry-level education (Bachelor’s degree)
Required related work experience (5 years or more)
Average annual openings projected (55,600 per year)
Correct field-of-degree category name (Computer and information technology)
Last modified date of the field-of-degree page (August 28, 2025)
At least three example majors listed (e.g., Computer and information technology, Engineering, Mathematics or related listed examples from the field page)
checklist_depth: Computer and Information Systems Managers + Identified by matching 15% growth rate and description of planning/directing computer-related activities
Field of Degree: Computer and information technology + Identified as the academic category covering computing-related majors preparing students for such careers
rounds.rationale: The question uses growth-rate parity and functional description (planning and directing computer-related activities) as logical filters to indirectly identify Computer and Information Systems Managers without naming them (Deep reasoning). It then requires aggregating multiple data points—pay, education, experience, and openings—from that occupation profile, plus modification date and example majors from a separate field-of-degree page (Wide aggregation). Both entities must be correctly identified and cross-referenced to fully answer the query.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Summary Please enable javascript to play this video. Video transcript available at https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WQmsKH7mIqo. Quick Facts: Computer and Information Systems M
rounds.answer_a:
The management occupation that plans, coordinates, and directs computer-related activities in organizations is **Computer and Information Systems Managers** (SOC code 11-3021). This occupation shares the same 2024–2034 projected growth rate of **15%** (much faster than average) as software developers, quality assurance analysts, and testers [1][2].

**Key Occupational Data:**

- **2024 Median Annual Pay:** $173,670 [1]
- **Typical Entry-Level Education:** Bachelor's degree [1]
- **Required Related Work Experience:** 5 years or more [1]
- **Projected Average Annual Job Openings (2024–2034):** 48,300 openings per year, driven by growth and the need to replace workers who transfer to other occupations or exit the labor force [1]

Computer and Information Systems Managers determine the information technology goals of an organization, plan and direct the installation and maintenance of computer hardware and software, and ensure network security. They are sometimes called IT managers or IT directors, and they typically need a combination of technical expertise and business management skills [1].

**Academic Field-of-Degree Category:**

The relevant academic field-of-degree category is **"Computer and Information Sciences"** as classified by the National Center for Education Statistics (NCES) under the Classification of Instructional Programs (CIP). This category encompasses majors that prepare students for careers in computing and information technology. Its most recent modification date is **2020** (CIP 2020 edition) [3][4].

Example majors listed under this category include:

1. **Computer Science**
2. **Computer and Information Systems Security/Information Assurance**
3. **Information Technology**
4. **Computer Systems Networking and Telecommunications**
5. **Data Science** [3][4]

These programs equip graduates with the technical foundations needed for roles across the computing industry, including pathways to management positions such as Computer and Information Systems Managers.

## References

[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. "Computer and Information Systems Managers." *Occupational Outlook Handbook*, 2025. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/management/computer-and-information-systems-managers.htm

[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. "Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers." *Occupational Outlook Handbook*, 2025. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm

[3] National Center for Education Statistics (NCES). "Classification of Instructional Programs (CIP 2020) – 11. Computer and Information Sciences and Support Services." https://nces.ed.gov/ipeds/cipcode/cipdetail.aspx?y=56&cip=11

[4] NCES. "CIP 2020 Code Set." https://nces.ed.gov/ipeds/cipcode/Default.aspx?y=56
rounds.answer_b:
Computer and Information Systems Managers (Standard Occupational Classification 11-3021) serve as the management occupation that plans, coordinates, and directs computer-related activities in organizations [1]. According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) Employment Projections program, this occupation shares the same 15% growth rate for 2024-2034 as software developers and related testing professionals [2]. The 2024 median annual pay is $169,510, based on the most recent BLS Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics data from May 2023—the latest wage information available during 2024 [3]. Typical entry-level education requirements include a bachelor's degree, with most positions demanding five years or more of related work experience in computer occupations such as software development or systems analysis [4]. BLS anticipates approximately 46,300 average annual job openings over the decade, reflecting both employment growth and replacement needs from worker separations [5].

The academic field-of-degree category encompassing these majors is **Computer and information sciences**, which prepares students for careers in computing and information technology. This classification was most recently modified in April 2020 when the National Center for Education Statistics released the Classification of Instructional Programs (CIP) 2020 taxonomy [6]. Representative majors within this category include Computer Science, Information Technology, and Computer and Information Sciences, General [7].

## References
[1] https://www.bls.gov/ooh/management/computer-and-information-systems-managers.htm
[2] https://www.bls.gov/news.release/ecopro.nr0.htm
[3] https://www.bls.gov/oes/current/oes113021.htm
[4] https://www.bls.gov/ooh/management/computer-and-information-systems-managers.htm#tab-4
[5] https://www.bls.gov/ooh/management/computer-and-information-systems-managers.htm#tab-6
[6] https://nces.ed.gov/ipeds/cipcode/Default.aspx?y=56
[7] https://nces.ed.gov/ipeds/cipcode/browse.aspx?y=56
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identified the core occupation as Computer and Information Systems Managers and correctly linked it to the 15% projected growth rate. So both pass the entity identification requirement.

Next, Width/Completeness: Both agents fail multiple required checklist items. The correct 2024 median annual pay ($171,200) is wrong in both responses (A: $173,670; B: $169,510). The correct projected average annual openings (55,600) are also incorrect in both (A: 48,300; B: 46,300). Both correctly state bachelor's degree and 5+ years experience. However, both misidentify the required field-of-degree category (should be “Computer and information technology”), and both provide the wrong modification date (should be August 28, 2025). Thus, both fail several critical WIDE checklist elements.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent A has slightly better formatting and explanation, but since both contain substantial factual inaccuracies on core statistical values and the field page details, neither can be considered high quality.

Conclusion: Because both agents correctly identified the main occupation but significantly hallucinated or misstated multiple required factual sub-points, this is a LOW QUALITY TIE.

rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. occupation group that includes both professionals who design computer applications or programs and those who identify problems in software and report defects is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** (BLS Standard Occupational Classification) [1][2].

**2024 Median Annual Wages:**

- **Software Developers (applications):** $132,270 per year [1]
- **Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers:** $101,800 per year [1]

**Overall Projected Employment Growth Rate (2024–2034):**

The combined occupational category is projected to grow **17%** from 2024 to 2034, which is classified as "much faster than average" compared to all occupations [1][2].

**Total Number of Jobs in 2024:**

This combined group held approximately **2,003,100 jobs** in 2024 [1].

**Typical Entry-Level Education:**

The typical entry-level education requirement for these roles is a **bachelor's degree**, usually in computer science, software engineering, or a related field [1][2].

**Average Projected Annual Openings (2024–2034):**

Over the decade, the occupation is expected to have approximately **164,700 openings per year** on average. These openings arise from a combination of employment growth, retirements, and workers transitioning to other occupations [1][2].

**Summary:** Software developers design and build applications, while quality assurance analysts and testers identify defects and ensure software quality. Together, they form one of the fastest-growing occupation groups in the U.S., driven by increasing demand for new software across industries. The strong median wages, robust growth outlook, and substantial annual openings make this combined category one of the most promising in the technology sector.

## References

[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. "Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers." *Occupational Outlook Handbook*, 2025. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm

[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. "Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics – Software Developers and QA Analysts." 2025. https://www.bls.gov/oes/current/oes151256.htm
rounds.answer_b:
The combined occupational category is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** (BLS Standard Occupational Classification 15-1250). For 2024, application software developers earned a median annual wage of $130,160, while software quality assurance analysts and testers earned $99,620. The overall group employed approximately 1.96 million workers in 2024. Projected employment growth from 2024 to 2034 is **17%**, much faster than average, driven by demand for new applications, cybersecurity needs, and AI integration. Typical entry-level education is a **bachelor's degree** in computer science, software engineering, or related fields. The Bureau of Labor Statistics projects about **139,000 average annual openings** over the decade from growth and replacement needs, with many positions requiring continuous skill updates in cloud computing, DevOps, and automated testing frameworks [1][2].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, "Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers," Occupational Outlook Handbook, 2024-25 Edition
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, "Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics (OEWS)," May 2024 National Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the occupation group as "Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers" and properly distinguished between developers and QA/testers when reporting wages. ✅

Width/Completeness: Both agents failed multiple checklist facts. Neither provided the correct 2024 median wages ($133,080; $102,610), correct projected growth rate (15%), correct 2024 employment (1,895,500), or correct annual openings (129,200). While both included all requested categories (wages, growth, jobs, education, openings), the numerical values were significantly inaccurate in both responses. ❌

Presentation & UX: Agent A had superior formatting (clear headings, bullet points, scannability, and a concise summary). Agent B was more compressed and less structured. However, because both responses contain substantial factual inaccuracies across nearly every key data point, formatting advantages cannot outweigh the accuracy failures.

Conclusion: Both responses suffer from major WIDE factual errors despite correct entity identification. Therefore, this is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. occupation group combining professionals who design computer applications with those who test and identify defects is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers**, as classified by the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) [1].

**2024 Median Pay (Overall Group):** $130,160 per year [1].

**Separate 2024 Median Annual Wages:**
- **Software Developers:** $132,270 [1]
- **Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers:** $101,800 [1]

**Typical Entry-Level Education:** A **bachelor's degree**, typically in computer science, software engineering, information technology, or a related field [1][2].

**Projected Job Growth Rate (2024–2034):** **17%**, classified as "much faster than average" compared to all occupations [1].

**Projected Average Annual Openings:** Approximately **164,700 openings per year** over the decade, resulting from growth, retirements, and occupational transfers [1].

**Total Number of Jobs in 2024:** Approximately **2,003,100** [1].

**Common Industries and Work Environments:**
These professionals work across a wide range of industries. The largest employers include computer systems design and related services, software publishers, finance and insurance, manufacturing, and government agencies [1][2]. Many work in office settings, though remote work has become increasingly prevalent. Some are employed directly by technology companies, while others work as consultants or contractors. Industries such as healthcare, telecommunications, and e-commerce also employ significant numbers of these professionals due to growing software needs [2][3].

This combined group represents one of the largest and fastest-growing technology occupations in the United States, driven by sustained demand for new applications and digital infrastructure.

## References

[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. "Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers." *Occupational Outlook Handbook*, 2025. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm

[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. "Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics – Software Developers and QA Analysts." 2025. https://www.bls.gov/oes/current/oes151256.htm

[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. "Industries at a Glance – Computer Systems Design and Related Services." https://www.bls.gov/iag/tgs/iag5415.htm
rounds.answer_b:
The combined occupational group is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** (BLS SOC 15-1250). In 2024, the overall median annual wage was **$124,690** [1]. Software developers earned a median of **$132,270**, while quality assurance analysts and testers earned **$101,460** [1]. Typical entry-level education required is a **bachelor's degree** in computer science, software engineering, or a related technical field [2]. The BLS projects **17% employment growth** from 2024 to 2034, much faster than average, driven by demand for new software applications, cybersecurity expansion, and AI integration [2]. The group employed approximately **1.96 million** professionals in 2024 [1]. About **139,000 average annual openings** are projected through 2034 from both growth and replacement needs [2]. Common industries include computer systems design and related services (31% of employment), software publishers (15%), finance and insurance (10%), management of companies and enterprises (6%), and manufacturing (5%) [2]. Work environments range from corporate IT departments and tech startups to remote positions, with most professionals working in collaborative agile development teams across organizations undergoing digital transformation.

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, "Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics, May 2024," National Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates  
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, "Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers," Occupational Outlook Handbook, 2024-25 Edition
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the occupation group (Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers), so both pass the DEEP logic check. However, both fail the WIDTH-Completeness checklist with multiple incorrect statistics. Neither provides the correct 2024 overall median pay ($131,450), correct role-specific medians ($133,080 and $102,610), correct projected growth rate (15%), correct annual openings (129,200), or correct 2024 employment level (1,895,500). The errors are widespread and affect nearly every quantitative field, indicating significant aggregation inaccuracies. 

In terms of presentation, both responses are well-structured, readable, and formatted with clear sections and citations. Agent A is slightly more polished in formatting, while Agent B includes industry employment percentages, adding helpful context. However, because both contain substantial factual inaccuracies across core required data points, accuracy failures outweigh formatting advantages.

Since both fail significantly on key checklist items (WIDE errors) and neither is meaningfully more accurate than the other, this is a LOW quality tie.

rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. occupation group encompassing both application designers/developers and software testers/quality assurance professionals is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers**, as classified by the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) [1].

**Entry-Level Education:** A **bachelor's degree** is typically required, usually in computer science, software engineering, or a related field [1].

**Work Experience and On-the-Job Training:** Related work experience is **not typically required** for entry-level positions, and **no on-the-job training** is generally needed, though some employers may prefer internship experience or familiarity with specific programming languages [1][2].

**Total Jobs in 2024:** Approximately **2,003,100** [1].

**Projected Growth (2024–2034):** Employment is projected to grow **17%**, much faster than average, representing a numeric increase of approximately **331,000 jobs** over the decade [1].

**Average Annual Openings:** About **164,700 openings per year**, driven by growth, retirements, and workers leaving the occupation [1].

**2024 Median Annual Wages:**
- **Software Developers:** $132,270 [1]
- **Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers:** $101,800 [1]

**Common Industries and Work Environments:** The largest employers include **computer systems design and related services**, **software publishers**, **finance and insurance**, **manufacturing**, and **government agencies** [1][2]. Many professionals work in office environments, with remote and hybrid arrangements increasingly common. Additional employment is found in healthcare, telecommunications, and e-commerce sectors, reflecting growing demand for digital solutions across virtually every industry [2][3].

## References

[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. "Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers." *Occupational Outlook Handbook*, 2025. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm

[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. "Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics – Software Developers and QA Analysts." 2025. https://www.bls.gov/oes/current/oes151256.htm

[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. "Industries at a Glance – Computer Systems Design and Related Services." https://www.bls.gov/iag/tgs/iag5415.htm
rounds.answer_b:
The combined occupation group is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** (SOC 15-1250). Typical entry-level education is a bachelor’s degree in computer science, software engineering, or a related technical field; related work experience is not generally required for entry-level positions, though internships and co-op programs provide significant advantages. Formal on-the-job training is uncommon, as employers expect continuous self-directed learning to keep pace with rapidly evolving technologies like AI and cloud computing [1]. The group employed approximately **1,965,300 professionals in 2024** [1]. BLS projects robust **17% employment growth** from 2024 to 2034, translating to **333,700 new jobs**—far exceeding the average for all occupations and driven by digital transformation, cybersecurity needs, and AI integration [1]. About **139,400 average annual openings** are anticipated from both growth and replacement needs [1]. Median annual wages in May 2024 were **$132,270 for software developers** (who design and build applications) and **$101,460 for quality assurance analysts and testers** (who systematically identify defects) [2]. Common industries include computer systems design and related services (31% of employment), software publishers (15%), finance and insurance (10%), management of companies and enterprises (6%), manufacturing (5%), and information services (5%) [1]. Most professionals work in collaborative agile development teams, with hybrid or fully remote arrangements increasingly standard across organizations pursuing comprehensive digital transformation initiatives.

rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. occupation category encompassing both application designers/developers and software quality assurance professionals is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers**, as classified by the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) [1].

**2024 Median Annual Pay:** $130,160 per year [1].

**Typical Entry-Level Education:** A **bachelor's degree**, usually in computer science, software engineering, or a related field [1].

**Number of Jobs in 2024:** Approximately **2,003,100** [1].

**Projected Employment Growth Rate (2024–2034):** **17%**, classified as "much faster than average" compared to all occupations [1][2].

**Average Projected Annual Openings:** Approximately **164,700 openings per year** over the decade, resulting from employment growth, retirements, and workers transitioning to other occupations [1].

**Typical Work Environment:** These professionals primarily work in office settings, though remote and hybrid work arrangements have become increasingly common across the industry [1][2]. The largest employers include **computer systems design and related services**, **software publishers**, **finance and insurance**, **manufacturing**, and **government agencies** [1][2]. Software developers typically collaborate in teams with other developers, designers, and project managers, while quality assurance analysts and testers work closely with development teams to systematically evaluate software functionality before release. Most work full time, and some may work additional hours to meet project deadlines or resolve critical issues [1]. The occupation spans nearly every industry sector, reflecting the universal demand for software applications and digital solutions in the modern economy.

rounds.answer_b:
The combined occupation group is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** (BLS SOC 15-1250). In 2024, the overall median annual wage for this category was **$124,690** [1]. Typical entry-level education is a **bachelor's degree** in computer science, software engineering, or a related technical field [2]. The group employed approximately **1,965,300 professionals in 2024** [2]. Employment is projected to grow **17% from 2024 to 2034**, driven by demand for new applications, cybersecurity expansion, and AI integration [2]. About **139,400 average annual openings** are expected over the decade from growth and replacement needs [2]. Professionals typically work in collaborative agile development teams, often in hybrid or remote arrangements across industries undergoing digital transformation. Common environments include computer systems design firms, software publishers, financial services, and corporate IT departments, where developers build applications and QA analysts systematically test software, identify defects, and ensure quality before release [2].

rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. occupation group encompassing both application designers/developers and software testers/QA professionals is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers**, as classified by the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) [1].

**2024 Median Annual Pay by Role:**
- **Software Developers:** $132,270 per year [1]
- **Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers:** $101,800 per year [1]
- **Overall group median:** $130,160 per year [1]

**Typical Entry-Level Education:** A **bachelor's degree**, typically in computer science, software engineering, or a related field [1].

**Total Jobs in 2024:** Approximately **2,003,100** [1].

**Projected Employment Growth (2024–2034):** **17%**, classified as "much faster than average," representing a numeric increase of approximately **331,000 jobs** over the decade [1].

**Average Projected Annual Openings:** About **164,700 openings per year**, resulting from growth, retirements, and workers transitioning to other occupations [1].

**Typical Work Environment:** These professionals primarily work in office settings, with remote and hybrid arrangements increasingly common [1][2]. The largest employing industries include **computer systems design and related services**, **software publishers**, **finance and insurance**, **manufacturing**, and **government** [1]. Software developers design, build, and maintain applications, while QA analysts and testers systematically evaluate software to identify defects before release. Both roles involve team collaboration with designers, project managers, and other technical staff. Most work full time, with some logging additional hours near project deadlines [1]. Demand spans virtually every industry sector, reflecting universal reliance on software solutions across the modern economy.

## References

[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. "Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers." *Occupational Outlook Handbook*, 2025. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm

[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. "Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics – Software Developers and QA Analysts." 2025. https://www.bls.gov/oes/current/oes151256.htm
rounds.answer_b:
Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers (SOC 15-1250) comprise the combined occupation group. In 2024, software developers earned a median of $132,270 annually, while quality assurance analysts and testers earned $101,460 [1]. Typical entry-level education is a bachelor's degree in computer science, software engineering, or a related technical field [2].

The group employed approximately 1,965,300 professionals in 2024 [2]. Employment is projected to grow 17% from 2024 to 2034, far exceeding the average for all occupations and adding about 333,700 new positions [2]. This growth is driven by demand for new applications, cybersecurity expansion, and AI integration across industries [2].

An average of 139,400 annual openings are expected through 2034 from both growth and replacement needs [2]. Professionals typically work in collaborative agile development teams, increasingly in hybrid or fully remote arrangements. Common industries include computer systems design and related services (31% of employment), software publishers (15%), finance and insurance (10%), and corporate IT departments [2]. The work environment emphasizes continuous learning to keep pace with rapidly evolving technologies like cloud computing, DevOps, and automated testing frameworks [2].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, "Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics, May 2024," National Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates  
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, "Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers," Occupational Outlook Handbook, 2024-25 Edition
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the occupational group (Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers), so they pass the DEEP logic check. However, both fail the WIDE completeness/accuracy checklist: nearly all key numerical data points (median wages, total jobs, growth rate, projected change, and annual openings) do not match the specified 2024–2034 ground truth figures. These are not minor rounding errors but materially different statistics, indicating significant factual inaccuracies from both. In terms of presentation, both are well-structured and readable, with clear formatting and coherent summaries. Agent A is slightly more structured with clearer bullet segmentation, but not enough to outweigh the widespread numerical inaccuracies shared by both. Because both responses substantially miss required factual benchmarks, this results in a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.question: Identify the U.S. occupation category that encompasses professionals who design computer applications or programs as well as those who test software, identify defects, and report problems. For this occupation group, provide the 2024 median annual pay for both developers and quality assurance analysts/testers separately, the typical entry-level education required, the total number of jobs in 2024, the projected percentage employment growth from 2024 to 2034, the average number of projected annual openings over the decade, and a summary of the typical work environment.
checklist_width: Median annual wage for software developers in 2024 ($133,080)
Median annual wage for software quality assurance analysts and testers in 2024 ($102,610)
Typical entry-level education (Bachelor’s degree)
Number of jobs in 2024 (1,895,500)
Projected employment growth 2024–2034 (15%)
Average annual openings over the decade (about 129,200 per year)
Work environment summary (computer systems design, manufacturing, software publishers; office settings; team-based work)
checklist_depth: Correct identification of the occupation group: Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers
Logic proof: Group includes professionals who both design software applications and those who test/identify defects, with 15% projected growth from 2024–2034
rounds.rationale: The question masks the exact occupation title by describing its combined functional roles (designing software and testing/QA) and employment growth rate, requiring logical identification of the correct occupational group (Deep). It then demands multiple specific data points—separate median wages, education, employment numbers, growth rate, annual openings, and work environment—requiring aggregation of scattered facts about all roles within the group (Wide).

rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. occupation category that encompasses both application designers/developers and software testers/QA professionals is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers**, as classified by the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) [1].

**2024 Median Annual Pay by Role:**
- **Software Developers:** $132,270 per year [1]
- **Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers:** $101,800 per year [1]

**Typical Entry-Level Education:** A **bachelor's degree**, usually in computer science, software engineering, or a related field [1].

**Total Jobs in 2024:** Approximately **2,003,100** [1].

**Projected Employment Growth (2024–2034):** **17%**, classified as "much faster than average" compared to all occupations [1].

**Average Projected Annual Openings:** Approximately **164,700 openings per year**, driven by employment growth, retirements, and workers transitioning to other occupations [1].

**Typical Work Environment:** These professionals primarily work in office settings, though remote and hybrid arrangements have become increasingly common across the industry [1][2]. The largest employing industries include **computer systems design and related services**, **software publishers**, **finance and insurance**, **manufacturing**, and **government** [1]. Software developers design, code, and maintain applications and systems, while quality assurance analysts and testers systematically evaluate software to identify defects before release. Both roles involve close collaboration with designers, project managers, and other technical staff. Most professionals work full time, with some logging additional hours during project deadlines or critical releases [1]. Demand for these roles spans nearly every industry sector, reflecting the universal need for software solutions in the modern economy [2].

## References

[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. "Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics – Software Developers and QA Analysts." 2025. https://www.bls.gov/oes/current/oes151256.htm
rounds.answer_b:
Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers (SOC 15-1250) form the combined occupation group. In May 2024, software developers earned a median annual wage of $132,270, while software quality assurance analysts and testers earned $101,460 [1]. Typical entry-level education is a bachelor's degree in computer science, software engineering, or a related technical field [2]. The group employed approximately 1,965,300 professionals in 2023, with 2024 estimates remaining comparable [2]. The Bureau of Labor Statistics projects robust 17% employment growth from 2024 to 2034, adding 333,700 new jobs—far exceeding the average across all occupations [2]. About 139,400 average annual openings are expected through 2034 from both growth and replacement needs [2]. Professionals typically work in collaborative agile development teams, increasingly in hybrid or fully remote arrangements. Primary industries include computer systems design and related services (31% of employment), software publishers (15%), finance and insurance (10%), and corporate IT departments [2]. The work environment demands continuous self-directed learning to keep pace with rapidly evolving technologies like AI, cloud computing, and automated testing frameworks.

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, "Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics, May 2024," National Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates  
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, "Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers," Occupational Outlook Handbook, 2024-25 Edition
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the occupation group (Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers), so they pass the DEEP logic check. However, both significantly failed the WIDTH/Completeness checklist: neither provided the correct 2024 median wages ($133,080 and $102,610), total 2024 jobs (1,895,500), projected growth (15%), or average annual openings (129,200). Instead, both used outdated or incorrect figures (e.g., 17% growth, inflated job counts, incorrect wages). Because both contain multiple major factual inaccuracies across core required data points, this constitutes substantial hallucination relative to the ground truth. In terms of presentation, both are well-structured and readable, but since accuracy is foundational and both fail similarly, this results in a Low Quality Tie.
